design_philosophy.core_loop: Physical mat-based input to control athlete speed and timing; compete in Olympic-inspired track and field events against computer or other players
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Rhythm execution and physical coordination—player must time foot strikes on the control mat to match visual cues (hurdle timing, jump markers) while maintaining peak velocity
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Competing as an Olympic athlete in track and field events; testing physical fitness and reflexes against computer opponents or friends
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Bridges arcade sports gaming with physical exergaming via proprietary mat accessory; emphasizes synchronization between body input and on-screen action rather than traditional controller abstraction. Tournament mode enforces penalty system (false starts disqualify), reinforcing discipline and precision over casual play.

difficulty.checkpointing: Event-based: tournament mode requires winning sequential races against six computer opponents; three false starts in a single event results in disqualification from that event
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited agency; difficulty is determined by computer opponent speed selection and event structure. Player can register up to six alternating players per event, allowing team participation but not difficulty adjustment.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Tournament mode: player must sequentially defeat six computer opponents in both 100M dash and 110M hurdles to win. Olympics mode allows all four events (100M dash, 110M hurdles, long jump, triple jump) with scoring benchmarked against 1982 Guinness world records
replayability_hooks: Multiplayer support (up to 2 simultaneous players on mat, up to 6 registered alternating players)
Multiple event types with distinct mechanics (dashing, hurdling, jumping)
Tournament and Olympics modes offer different progression structures
Competitive scoring against world records and computer opponents

world_building_techniques: Olympic theming: stadium visuals, track and field event naming conventions
Computer opponent names (tournament mode shows named competitors)
Regional adaptation: Japanese flags replaced with US flags in localized versions, tournament opponent visual design changed

faction_indicators: Regional identity through localization: Japanese (Running Stadium) vs. North American (Stadium Events/World Class Track Meet) branding
Olympic theming signals international competitive sport context
danger_signals: False start penalty system enforces rule compliance and physical discipline
Disqualification after three false starts creates high-stakes tournament pressure
environmental_storytelling: Stadium setting (visual background, colored audience seating)
Track and field event locations signal competitive athletic environment
Mat accessory (Family Fun Fitness/Power Pad) grounds gameplay in physical fitness culture of mid-1980s

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Family Fun Fitness: Stadium Events
